policy_statement: Automate and make water consumption measurement hour-based and remotely readable; HSY recommends consumption-based invoicing.
mechanism_description: Automating hourly, remotely readable water metering and recommending consumption-based invoicing improves consumption visibility and price signals, supporting reduced water use.
primary_causal_pathway: Same water service → ↓ water demand → ↓ energy for pumping/treatment/heating → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Deploy hourly, remotely readable smart meters and pair them with consumption feedback and/or consumption-based pricing/billing to influence user behavior and utility operations (e.g., leak detection, demand management). (2) Mediating process: Improved visibility and price signals reduce discretionary use and accelerate leak identification/repair, lowering total volumes conveyed and treated. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced electricity/heat set for abstraction, treatment, distribution, wastewater collection/treatment, and in many cases reduced end-use hot-water heating, yielding lower GHG emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced energy use (and associated GHG emissions) across water supply/wastewater systems—and often hot-water heating—due to lower per-capita water consumption.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism reduces the quantity of water demanded via measurement/feedback and pricing signals, which in turn reduces energy use in water and wastewater systems (and potentially hot-water heating), lowering emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While there is a mitigation co-benefit (↓ GHG), the core mechanism is delivering the same service (water supply) with less resource throughput—i.e., efficiency—so Resource Efficiency best captures the dominant pathway common to all such policies.  
3) Edge cases applied: In drought-prone contexts, the same mechanism can also support adaptation (reduced water stress), but that is not the single biggest climate-relevant effect universally; the consistent cross-context climate linkage is reduced energy/emissions from lower water volumes.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy that implements smart metering + consumption-based billing/feedback aims to reduce per-capita water use through information and price signals, producing the same efficiency-led pathway even if local energy intensity of water varies.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether the resulting benefit is mainly utility-side electricity savings, customer hot-water energy savings, or adaptation benefits depends on local system characteristics and is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the jurisdiction’s water supply/wastewater energy intensity is very low (e.g., gravity-fed, low-treatment) and hot-water impacts are minimal, climate mitigation benefits may be small and the policy may function more as water-security adaptation; however, the mechanism as defined is still primarily an efficiency-driven demand-reduction pathway.
key_indicators: "automated"; "hour-based"; "remotely readable"; "measurement of water consumption"; "recommends consumption-based invoicing"
co_benefits: Reduced non-revenue water/leak losses; improved billing accuracy and fairness; operational efficiencies for the utility; customer awareness/behavior change; potential affordability impacts (positive or negative) depending on tariff design.
instance_edge_case_notes: “HSY recommends consumption-based invoicing” points toward a pricing/billing reform (pricing-mechanism), but because it is phrased as a recommendation and the operative commitment is metering automation/remote readability, the instrument is classified as infrastructure-investment/enabling rather than a pricing mechanism.
